critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Late-July grazer moving through tall green cover while fawns grow bolder and velvet antlers gain serious size.
critters.fact: By late July, white-tailed fawns are increasingly mobile, while bucks continue rapidly growing antlers beneath sensitive velvet.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer may feed in clover, fields, and trail openings during cooler hours before retreating into shade.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Heavy summer forager following berries, insects, and ripening food through shaded woods and wetland edges.
critters.fact: Late July brings expanding berry and fruit crops that help black bears steadily add weight before autumn.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bears may visit berry patches, rotting logs, swamp edges, and quiet trails connecting reliable feeding areas.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Summer family provider covering trails and field margins while nearly grown pups sharpen their hunting skills.
critters.fact: By late July, coyote pups spend more time outside den areas, playing, exploring, and learning to hunt with adults.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes may travel in small family groups along two-tracks, fields, and brush lines near dawn or dusk.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Rust-colored edge hunter guiding increasingly independent young through tall grass and rodent-rich openings.
critters.fact: Young red foxes become increasingly independent during midsummer as they practice hunting insects, rodents, and other small prey.
critters.activeFact: Right now, foxes may appear near mowed lanes, field margins, and grassy pockets where young animals can practice hunting.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Secretive summer fox staying buried in brush while young family members begin exploring beyond familiar cover.
critters.fact: Gray fox young become more mobile during summer but continue relying on thick woods and brush for safety.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray foxes are most likely along tangled edges, wooded lanes, and shaded cover during darker hours.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Leaf-shadow hunter using heavy summer vegetation to slip close to rabbits, rodents, and young woodland prey.
critters.fact: Bobcats hunt effectively in dense summer cover by following game trails, brush lines, and natural travel funnels.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may patrol quiet crossings and transition zones where prey moves between shade and feeding areas.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow summer browser working leafy branches, ground plants, and mineral-rich roadside snacks at its own speed.
critters.fact: Porcupines eat leaves, herbs, twigs, bark, and aquatic vegetation, and may seek salty objects during summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, porcupines may feed in leafy trees, roadside vegetation, or damp areas with abundant green plants.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Night-shift wetland engineer maintaining dams and channels as summer heat and lower water test the whole system.
critters.fact: During late summer, beavers repair leaks and adjust dams to preserve water depth around lodges and feeding areas.
critters.activeFact: Right now, newly packed mud, fresh cuttings, and reinforced dam sections may reveal recent overnight work.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Warm-water marsh commuter weaving through thick cattails and feeding lanes hidden beneath summer vegetation.
critters.fact: Muskrats feed heavily on cattails, pondweed, sedges, and other aquatic plants during the growing season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may appear briefly in open water before disappearing into dense shoreline plants.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Fast shoreline raider hunting frogs, crayfish, fish, and careless prey along warm midsummer water.
critters.fact: Minks use narrow shoreline corridors to surprise aquatic and terrestrial prey during summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink may dart along muddy banks, fallen logs, and creek mouths where prey gathers near water.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Hidden forest traveler moving through deep leafy timber where shade and thick cover keep sightings rare.
critters.fact: Fishers remain active throughout summer but often travel beneath dense forest canopy where they are difficult to observe.
critters.activeFact: Right now, trail cameras along wooded ridges and old logging routes may offer the best chance of detecting one.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Playful aquatic hunter cruising warm channels where fish, frogs, crayfish, and muddy slides stay productive.
critters.fact: River otters may travel several miles of connected shoreline while hunting during open-water seasons.
critters.activeFact: Right now, otters may leave tracks, slides, scat, or disturbed shallows along ponds, creeks, and riverbanks.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Midsummer canopy acrobat feeding through shade while young squirrels learn every shortcut to the feeders.
critters.fact: By late July, young gray squirrels are increasingly independent and spend more time foraging away from nests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels may raid feeders early, then retreat into leafy shade during the hottest hours.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny conifer landlord monitoring summer cone crops and yelling eviction notices at everything that moves.
critters.fact: Red squirrels track developing cone crops closely and begin harvesting or caching food as seeds mature.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may chatter from conifers, cut green cones, and defend feeding areas with maximum volume.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Striped summer courier hauling seeds, fruit, and crumbs through shaded routes with cheek pouches fully operational.
critters.fact: Chipmunks gather summer seeds and fruits while maintaining underground food stores and sleeping chambers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks may make repeated runs between feeding spots and burrows during cooler parts of the day.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Brown-coated forest hare disappearing into thick understory while browsing leaves, grasses, and tender summer plants.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares rely on dense young forest and shrub growth for both summer food and protection from predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hares may remain nearly invisible in shaded brush unless they bolt across a trail or opening.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Brush-loving summer rabbit feeding through green abundance while young litters add extra movement along the edges.
critters.fact: Eastern cottontails may raise several litters during the warm season when food and protective vegetation are abundant.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults and young rabbits may feed around lawns, clover, gardens, and brush piles at dawn or dusk.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Summer night burglar teaching young raccoons the sacred arts of creek patrol, feeder inspection, and lid removal.
critters.fact: By late July, young raccoons often travel with their mother while learning where and how to find food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, family groups may visit pond edges, feeders, fruit trees, campsites, and unsecured containers after dark.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Warm-night cleanup specialist wandering through insects, fallen fruit, carrion, and leftovers across summer edges.
critters.fact: Opossums take advantage of summer insects, fruits, carrion, eggs, and other foods encountered during nightly travel.
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may patrol under fruiting plants, around buildings, or along brushy routes after sunset.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Striped summer excavator rooting through lawns and fields while young skunks learn the family fragrance business.
critters.fact: Young striped skunks often begin accompanying their mother on feeding trips during midsummer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, small skunk groups may forage for grubs and insects along lawns, trails, and damp field margins.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Silent canopy glider moving through warm leafy nights where seeds, fungi, insects, and fruit fill the menu.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels supplement seeds and nuts with insects, fungi, fruit, and other seasonal summer foods.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nighttime cameras or feeders may catch quick glides and brief visits beneath the full canopy.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Small aerial insect hunter working peak summer bug traffic over ponds, clearings, and woodland corridors.
critters.fact: Female little brown bats gather in maternity colonies where young begin flying during midsummer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bats may make repeated feeding passes over water and openings shortly after sunset.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Broad-winged summer hunter circling lights and clearings where beetles and moths keep the night buffet stocked.
critters.fact: Big brown bat pups begin flying during summer and must quickly learn to hunt insects using echolocation.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults and newly flying young may hunt near buildings, tree gaps, lights, and pond edges.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Leaf-hidden woodland owl guiding fledged young through summer hunting lessons beneath the dark canopy.
critters.fact: Young barred owls may remain near their parents for months after leaving the nest while learning to hunt.
critters.activeFact: Right now, begging calls from young owls may reveal a family hidden in dense woods after dusk.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Powerful summer owl supervising nearly independent young that still expect dinner delivered with talons.
critters.fact: Fledged great horned owls continue relying on their parents for food while developing stronger hunting skills.
critters.activeFact: Right now, young owls may call repeatedly after dark while adults hunt across fields and woodland edges.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Loud summer canopy boss escorting young birds through feeders, branches, and neighborhood security drills.
critters.fact: Young blue jays remain with family groups after fledging and may noisily follow adults while learning to forage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jay families may crowd feeders, call constantly, and chase anything entering their favorite trees.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Red summer vocalist still defending brush territory while pairs raise young or begin another nesting attempt.
critters.fact: Northern cardinals commonly raise more than one brood during the breeding season when conditions remain favorable.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults may carry insects or seeds to young while males continue singing from shaded perches.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny woodland guide moving through leafy family groups as fledglings learn where every insect and feeder lives.
critters.fact: After leaving the nest, young chickadees often travel with adults and other woodland birds while learning to forage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, loose chickadee family groups may sweep through branches, feeders, and shaded forest edges.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Gray-crested summer chatterbox guiding fledglings through branches, feeders, and proper seed-cracking technique.
critters.fact: Young tufted titmice may remain with their parents after fledging and join mixed woodland feeding groups.
critters.activeFact: Right now, titmouse families may move noisily through shady trees and make repeated feeder visits.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Upside-down trunk inspector leading young birds through the ancient family art of walking the wrong direction.
critters.fact: Young nuthatches follow adults after fledging while learning to search bark for insects and hidden food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, family groups may creep around mature trunks, call softly, and visit feeders together.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small bark specialist now escorting young woodpeckers through suet stops and summer insect patrol.
critters.fact: Young downy woodpeckers may follow adults after fledging while learning to probe bark and locate insects.
critters.activeFact: Right now, downy families may move through small branches, visit suet, and call back and forth.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Large trunk worker teaching young birds how to turn hidden beetle larvae into a proper summer meal.
critters.fact: Fledged hairy woodpeckers continue learning how to excavate insects from bark and decaying wood.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults and young may work mature trees together, producing scattered tapping and sharp calls.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Giant forest carpenter leading young through ant-filled timber while leaving wood chips like construction confetti.
critters.fact: Young pileated woodpeckers remain with their parents after fledging while learning to locate carpenter ants and beetle larvae.
critters.activeFact: Right now, family groups may call loudly and work dead or damaged trees across shaded forest territory.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Noisy summer woodpecker guiding young through tree trunks, feeders, fruit, and proper territorial complaining.
critters.fact: Red-bellied woodpecker fledglings may follow adults to feeding areas while learning to find insects, fruit, nuts, and seeds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, family groups may call around feeders and tree trunks as young birds practice independent feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced serial nester still balancing fledglings, new eggs, and structurally optimistic twig platforms.
critters.fact: Mourning doves may begin another nesting attempt soon after earlier young leave the nest.
critters.activeFact: Right now, doves may feed fledglings on the ground while another pair carries twigs nearby.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Bright yellow late-season nester now getting serious as thistle, milkweed, and summer seeds reach prime condition.
critters.fact: American goldfinches begin nesting later than most local songbirds, often during July when plant fibers and seeds are abundant.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pairs may gather nesting material, chase through open areas, and feed heavily on developing seeds.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-country summer resident mostly absent from lower feeders while breeding in northern or higher-elevation habitat.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed juncos that remain in Michigan during summer generally favor cooler northern forests or elevated terrain.
critters.activeFact: Right now, juncos may be uncommon around regular feeders unless suitable cool breeding habitat is nearby.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social summer singer juggling fledglings, repeat nesting, and steady feeder traffic around human neighborhoods.
critters.fact: House finches may raise multiple broods and frequently reuse sheltered structures or nearby areas for additional nests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults may feed noisy fledglings, sing from exposed perches, and make constant seed runs.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy northern breeder moving quietly through conifers and mixed woods while summer foliage hides the whole operation.
critters.fact: Purple finches breed in northern woodland and often place nests in conifers or dense tree cover.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may remain difficult to spot unless songs or feeder visits reveal them near forest habitat.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Hen-led summer family caravan moving poults through insects, seeds, short grass, and protective edge cover.
critters.fact: By late July, turkey poults are stronger flyers but still rely heavily on hens for protection and guidance.
critters.activeFact: Right now, broods may feed through fields, trails, and woodland openings before vanishing into cover when disturbed.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Quiet forest family moving through thick ground cover while young grouse grow feathers and stronger escape skills.
critters.fact: Ruffed grouse chicks grow rapidly during summer and shift from insect-heavy diets toward more plant material.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hens and young may remain hidden in dense understory, old clearings, and brushy forest edges.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall wetland family guiding growing colts through fields and marshes with unmistakable prehistoric commentary.
critters.fact: Sandhill crane colts grow quickly through summer but remain with their parents while learning feeding and danger awareness.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crane families may forage in open fields, wetlands, and shallow water while adults stay alert.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Black-feathered family patrol teaching young crows where to find food, spot danger, and complain professionally.
critters.fact: Young American crows often remain with family groups after fledging and learn through close observation of adults.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crow families may gather around fields, roads, woods, and feeding areas with plenty of loud discussion.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Large summer corvid traveling in family groups while young ravens practice calls, flight, and intelligent troublemaking.
critters.fact: Young ravens may remain with adults through summer while learning complex feeding and social behaviors.
critters.activeFact: Right now, raven families may soar over timber, investigate food sources, and exchange a wide variety of calls.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Broad-winged summer hunter coaching fledglings while field rodents become unwilling training equipment.
critters.fact: Young red-tailed hawks leave the nest during summer but continue depending on adults while learning to hunt.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fledglings may call from trees, make awkward flights, and wait for adults to deliver prey.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland predator raising young in leafy cover where every songbird feeder doubles as a hunting classroom.
critters.fact: Cooper’s hawk fledglings begin leaving nests in summer but remain nearby while adults continue providing food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, young hawks may call from woods while adults make quick low hunting flights through trees.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Warm-water pond regular guiding larger ducklings while adult males slip into low-profile summer molt.
critters.fact: Male mallards molt into dull eclipse plumage during summer, while ducklings continue growing flight feathers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mallards may loaf in shaded water, feed along vegetation, or travel with nearly grown broods.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Shy backwater duck hiding larger broods in shaded timber while adults begin looking less dressed for the gala.
critters.fact: Wood duck adults molt after breeding, and young birds continue developing flight feathers through midsummer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, family groups may stay concealed in flooded timber, shaded pond edges, and quiet creek bends.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Summer goose family marching nearly grown goslings between lawns and water with full neighborhood ownership energy.
critters.fact: By late July, Canada goose goslings are approaching adult size and developing the flight feathers needed for migration.
critters.activeFact: Right now, goose families may graze heavily and gather near open water as young birds prepare for flight.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Tall summer fisherman stalking shrinking shallows where warm water concentrates frogs, fish, and unfortunate snacks.
critters.fact: As summer water levels fall, great blue herons often benefit from prey becoming concentrated in shallower areas.
critters.activeFact: Right now, herons may hunt pond margins, creek pools, marsh openings, and exposed mudflats.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Sun-powered pond resident rotating between basking logs, warm water, and vegetation-rich feeding zones.
critters.fact: Painted turtles eat aquatic plants, insects, snails, small fish, and carrion during warm summer months.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may bask during cooler morning sun before feeding and sheltering in water as temperatures rise.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Heavy summer ambush turtle settled back into warm ponds after the season’s overland nesting journeys.
critters.fact: By late July, most female snapping turtles have completed nesting and returned to aquatic habitat.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snapping turtles may rest beneath vegetation or ambush prey in warm, shallow, murky water.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Warm-water panfish shifting between lingering nest duty, weed edges, shade, and summer feeding schools.
critters.fact: Bluegill may spawn repeatedly through summer when water temperatures remain favorable, with males guarding shallow nests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bluegill may occupy beds, docks, weed lines, and shaded structure depending on heat and spawning stage.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Heat-savvy ambush predator holding near weeds, shade, wood, and deeper edges during bright summer days.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass often seek shade or deeper structure during daytime heat and move shallower during cooler periods.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may hunt near weeds and shoreline cover early or late, then retreat deeper at midday.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Striped summer schooler following cooler water, weed lines, and roaming food beneath the warm surface.
critters.fact: Yellow perch often move toward deeper or cooler water as summer temperatures rise.
critters.activeFact: Right now, schools may gather near weed edges, drop-offs, and deeper structure where oxygen and food remain favorable.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Toothy ambush predator balancing weedy hunting lanes with a midsummer preference for cooler water.
critters.fact: Northern pike prefer cooler conditions and may retreat toward deeper water or shaded vegetation during summer heat.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may hold along deep weed edges, creek mouths, shaded cover, and cooler ambush routes.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny former spring headliner now hiding quietly in damp woods while summer frogs control the evening soundtrack.
critters.fact: After breeding season, spring peepers spend summer in moist woodland vegetation rather than open water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, peepers are usually quiet and hidden in shaded plants, wet woods, and humid low areas.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big pond vocalist booming through warm July nights while guarding prime shoreline calling stations.
critters.fact: Male bullfrogs defend territories during summer breeding season and use deep calls to attract females.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs may call loudly after dark, sit along warm banks, and chase rivals from favorite spots.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline banjo player fully active around summer ponds, ditches, marshes, and shaded water edges.
critters.fact: Green frogs continue breeding through summer, with males calling from territories near shallow water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, their single-note plunks may sound from weedy banks and quiet pond margins throughout the evening.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted summer jumper roaming wet grass and meadow edges farther from water than many pond-loving frogs.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs often forage in moist grasslands and meadows during summer after completing spring breeding.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may leap from damp grass, puddled trails, marsh edges, and shaded vegetation after rain.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Warm-season hunter cruising grass and wet edges for frogs, worms, slugs, and other bite-sized opportunities.
critters.fact: Garter snakes may be pregnant during summer, with females giving birth to live young later in the season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may bask briefly in morning sun before hunting shaded grass, gardens, and wetland margins.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Heavy-bodied shoreline hunter basking between fishing trips through warm ponds, creeks, and marsh channels.
critters.fact: Water snakes hunt fish and amphibians and often rest on branches, rocks, or banks close to water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may bask during cooler hours and retreat into water or shade during intense midday heat.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive summer rodent hunter using barns, boards, rocks, and shaded cover as daytime hideouts.
critters.fact: Eastern milk snakes often avoid extreme daytime heat by sheltering beneath objects and becoming active during cooler hours.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may hide under boards or rocks by day and hunt rodents around edges after temperatures ease.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Orange summer traveler moving between milkweed patches while caterpillars quietly chew through the family business.
critters.fact: Several monarch generations move north and reproduce during summer, with caterpillars feeding only on milkweed.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults, eggs, or striped caterpillars may appear around milkweed and nearby nectar flowers.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Large midsummer butterfly sailing between gardens, wildflowers, and muddy mineral stops in full winged elegance.
critters.fact: Male swallowtails may gather on damp soil to absorb salts and minerals in a behavior called puddling.
critters.activeFact: Right now, swallowtails may visit flowers, sunny trails, wet soil, and garden edges during calm weather.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Peak-season aerial interceptor patrolling ponds and fields where mosquitoes and flies enter restricted airspace.
critters.fact: Dragonflies catch mosquitoes, flies, and other insects in flight using their legs like a capture basket.
critters.activeFact: Right now, dragonflies may defend pond territories, hover over trails, and chase rivals through sunny openings.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Slender summer flyer threading through shoreline plants where calm water and tiny insect prey stay abundant.
critters.fact: Adult damselflies hunt small insects and often remain close to the aquatic habitat where they developed.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may perch on reeds, grasses, and pond plants between short hunting flights.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Midsummer lantern crew still blinking through humid grass and woodland edges, though peak glow may begin easing.
critters.fact: Firefly flashing activity varies by species and weather, with warm humid evenings usually producing stronger displays.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fireflies may flash after sunset over tall grass, damp lowlands, gardens, and forest margins.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Midsummer flower worker hauling nectar and pollen through clover, gardens, meadows, and blooming field edges.
critters.fact: Honeybee colonies reach high activity during summer as workers gather food and regulate hive temperature.
critters.activeFact: Right now, honeybees may crowd productive flowers and carry pollen loads back to the hive throughout the day.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Fuzzy colony workforce operating at summer strength while queens stay home and workers handle the flower routes.
critters.fact: By late July, established bumblebee colonies contain many workers gathering nectar and pollen for developing young.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bumblebees may work clover, gardens, berries, wildflowers, and flowering shrubs from morning into evening.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Late-July jumper growing larger, louder, and more noticeable through sunny grasslands and crunchy trail edges.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers grow through several immature stages called nymphal instars before becoming winged adults.
critters.activeFact: Right now, larger grasshoppers may jump from warm grass, dry fields, gardens, and sunny trail margins.
